data:
A rectangular white marble urn, with a lid. The lid has
                                            acroteria on each of the four corners. The urn has square fluted columns
                                            depicted in relief on each vertical corner, and a tabula ansata in light
                                            relief fills the front face.

Greek text over four lines, centred in the main panel of the
                                        tabula ansata on the front face of the urn.

Neat and regular square letters. Phi and omicron are rhomboid in
                                    form; standard four-bar sigma; epsilon has horizontal bars of equal length; alpha
                                    has a slightly extended apex; mu has extended apices, and all four strokes are full
                                    length and off-vertical. There is one instance of ligature in line 3, and
                                    interpuncts are in the form of simple triangular incisions.
69 CE — first half of 2nd century CE

Found in 1913 in a Roman tomb uncovered during building works in prop. Guglielmino, in the area known as Orto del Re (probably the insula bounded by via Nino Martoglio, via Stellata, via Orto San Clemente, and via Reclusorio del Lume).

I.Sicily with the permission of the Assessorato Regionale dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana - Dipartimento dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana (Photo by students of the Liceo Lazzaro)
Castello Ursino, Piazza Federico II di Svevia, 95121 Catania (CT)
Le iscrizioni del Museo civico di Catania : storia delle collezioni, cultura epigrafica, edizione
Kalle Korhonen, Le iscrizioni del Museo civico di Catania : storia delle collezioni, cultura epigrafica, edizione (Helsinki: Societas Scientiarum Fennica, 2004)

Accademia nazionale dei Lincei, Istituto nazionale di archeologia e storia dell’arte (Italy), e Reale Accademia d’Italia, «Notizie degli scavi di antichità», Notizie degli Scavi di Antichità, 1876, http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1646037

ISic003236. Inscribed funerary urn of Flavianos

Text encoding: Jonathan Prag
1Φλαβιανὸς · χρησ-
2τὸς · καὶ ἄμεν(πτος) · ἔζη(σεν) ·
3ἔτη · λζ´ · μῆ͡ν(ας) · γ´ ·
4ἡμέ(ρας) · ι´ ·

Text encoding: Victoria Fendel, Robert Crellin
Φλαβιανὸς χρηστὸς καὶ ἄμενπτος ἔζησεν ἔτη λζ μῆνας γ ἡμέρας ι

Text encoding: Jonathan Prag
1ΦΛΑΒΙΑΝΟΣ·ΧΡΗΣ
2ΤΟΣ·ΚΑΙΑΜΕΝ·ΕΖΗ·
3ΕΤΗ·ΛΖ·ΜΗ͡Ν·Γ·
4ΗΜΕ·Ι·

Text encoding: Victoria Fendel, Robert Crellin
ΦΛΑΒΙΑΝΟΣΧΡΗΣΤΟΣΚΑΙΑΜΕΝΠΤΟΣΕΖΗΣΕΝΕΤΗΛΖΜΗΝΑΣΓΗΜΕΡΑΣΙ

Facsimile Images

Apparatus criticus

  • Text from autopsy (Prag)

English translation

Jonathan Prag

Phlabianos (Flavianus), worthy and blameless, lived for 37 years, 3 months, 10 days.

Italian translation

Serena Agodi

Flaviano, degno e innocente, visse 37 anni, 3 mesi, 10 giorni.

commentary

According to Paolo Orsi, the urn still contained the deceased’s ashes when it was discovered, and traces of gilding were still visible on some of the letters and the capital of one of the relief columns. The use of the name Flavianus suggests a date after the beginning of the Flavian dynasty (beginning with Vespasian, 69 AD), but Flavianus’ status as slave, freedman, or free remains uncertain. The use of architectural elements in the form of the chest and a tabula ansata to frame the inscription are typical features of ash chests of this period.

bibliography

  • Orsi NSA 1915 216 https://biblio.inscriptiones.org/epig10001392
  • Libertini 1937 50 https://biblio.inscriptiones.org/epig10001867
  • Wilson 1990 134 https://biblio.inscriptiones.org/epig10001509
  • Korhonen 2004 87 https://biblio.inscriptiones.org/epig10000028

Neat and regular square letters. Phi and omicron are rhomboid in form; standard four-bar sigma; epsilon has horizontal bars of equal length; alpha has a slightly extended apex; mu has extended apices, and all four strokes are full length and off-vertical. There is one instance of ligature in line 3, and interpuncts are in the form of simple triangular incisions.

Line 1-4 15-20 Not measured

Text from autopsy (Prag)

Phlabianos (Flavianus), worthy and blameless, lived for 37 years, 3 months, 10 days.

Flaviano, degno e innocente, visse 37 anni, 3 mesi, 10 giorni.

According to Paolo Orsi, the urn still contained the deceased’s ashes when it was discovered, and traces of gilding were still visible on some of the letters and the capital of one of the relief columns. The use of the name Flavianus suggests a date after the beginning of the Flavian dynasty (beginning with Vespasian, 69 AD), but Flavianus’ status as slave, freedman, or free remains uncertain. The use of architectural elements in the form of the chest and a tabula ansata to frame the inscription are typical features of ash chests of this period.
